column1: I.                    PRELIMINARIES
1.1. Could you please clarify the intended purpose, expected outcomes and specific outputs of the system or service of which AI techniques are an integral part of?
column2: Yes — ML used for receipt extraction, classification, anomaly detection, invoice capture, audit checks, and certain generative?AI features via SAP BTP.
column3: Joule (SAP Concur) uses machine learning to perform receipt/invoice/document extraction (OCR/NLP), field classification, anomaly detection, expense/invoice capture, audit checks and selected productivity features (assistive generative capabilities). Expected outputs are structured expense/invoice data (extracted fields such as date, merchant, amounts), classification labels, anomaly flags, and metadata to support AXA workflows and downstream processing.
column23: Yes — ML used for receipt extraction, classification, anomaly detection, invoice capture, audit checks, and certain generative?AI features via SAP BTP.

column1:
1.2. What role(s) does your organisation play in the AI supply chain for the service/product being provided?
(Select all that apply)
AI Provider:  A natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that develops an AI system or a general-purpose AI model, or has one developed, and places it on the market or puts it into service under its own name or trademark, whether for payment or free of charge.
AI Deployer:  A natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body using an AI system under its authority, except where the AI system is used in the course of a personal non-professional activity.
AI Importer:  A natural or legal person located or established in the Union that places on the market an AI system that bears the name or trademark of a natural or legal person established in a third country.
AI Distributor:  A natural or legal person in the supply chain, other than the provider or the importer, that makes an AI system available on the Union market.
Downstream Provider:  A provider of an AI system, including a general-purpose AI system, which integrates an AI model, regardless of whether the AI model is provided by themselves and vertically integrated or provided by another entity based on contractual relation.
column2: No - AI Provider
column3:
AI Provider — SAP Concur develops and operates Joule (the AI system) and places it into service under SAP Concur branding.
AI Deployer — SAP Concur deploys and operates the system for customers (including hosting, maintenance and operational support).
Not an AI Importer or Distributor by default for AXA engagements. If required by contract, reseller/distributor arrangements would be documented.

column2: No - AI Deployer
No - AI Importer
No - AI Distributor
No - Downstream Provider

column1: 1.10. Is personal data processed at any stage of the AI system's lifecycle, such as during system training, proof of concept or deployment?
column2: Yes
column3: When AXA uses Joule for production inference, personal data contained in receipts/invoices may be processed. Processing is performed under AXA’s instructions and governed by the DPA.
column23: Yes

column1:
1.10.1. If yes, please specify each category of personal data used and how it is handled in each phase, paying particular attention to special category data as it is defined in Art. 9 of GDPR.
Personal Data means “any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person (‘data subject’); an identifiable natural person is one who can be identified, directly or indirectly, in particular by reference to an identifier such as a name, an identification number, location data, an online identifier or to one or more factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural or social identity of that natural person”
column2:
Categories include payer/payee names, contact details, invoice/receipt line items, transaction dates, merchant names, transaction amounts and any metadata submitted by AXA. Handling:
Ingestion: secured pipeline with transport encryption.
Processing: inference (OCR/NLP) in segregated environments; PII detection and configurable redaction/pseudonymization applied before analytics or reuse.
Storage: encrypted at rest in access?controlled stores.
Logging: inputs are hashed or redacted in logs where feasible; decision metadata (model version, confidence) retained for audit.
Retention/deletion: per AXA’s contractual retention schedule and DPA.
Special category data: not processed unless explicitly agreed and legally permitted; would require specific contractual and legal controls.
column23:
Categories include payer/payee names, contact details, invoice/receipt line items, transaction dates, merchant names, transaction amounts and any metadata submitted by AXA. Handling:
Ingestion: secured pipeline with transport encryption.
Processing: inference (OCR/NLP) in segregated environments; PII detection and configurable redaction/pseudonymization applied before analytics or reuse.
Storage: encrypted at rest in access?controlled stores.
Logging: inputs are hashed or redacted in logs where feasible; decision metadata (model version, confidence) retained for audit.
Retention/deletion: per AXA’s contractual retention schedule and DPA.
Special category data: not processed unless explicitly agreed and legally permitted; would require specific contractual and legal controls.

All processing is governed by the DPA and technical organizational measures.

column1: II.                    PROHIBITED AI SYSTEM

2.1. Would your AI system, or the purposes to which any AI technique used for the provision of a service to AXA, fall under the category of Prohibited Artificial Intelligence Practices as defined in Art. 5 of the EU AI Act and associated Recitals, regardless of whether such system or technique is located or deployed in the EU?
column2: No. Joule’s functions (document extraction, classification, anomaly detection and assistive productivity) do not fall under Art.5 prohibited categories (e.g., subliminal manipulation, social scoring, remote biometric identification for law enforcement). Joule is not used for prohibited purposes.
column23: No. Joule’s functions (document extraction, classification, anomaly detection and assistive productivity) do not fall under Art.5 prohibited categories (e.g., subliminal manipulation, social scoring, remote biometric identification for law enforcement). Joule is not used for prohibited purposes.

column1: IV.                    AI MATURITY AND LITERACY
4.1 Regarding the development and maintenance of the proposed AI system/AI techniques:
4.1.1 What type of training has your organization undertaken for the development and maintenance of the AI system?
column2: Role?based training covering secure SDLC/DevSecOps, privacy and GDPR awareness, privacy?by?design, responsible AI principles (bias/fairness, interpretability), ML validation and monitoring practices, incident response for AI, and operational security. Technical training for ML engineers covers model interpretability, adversarial robustness and data?quality controls.
column23: Role?based training covering secure SDLC/DevSecOps, privacy and GDPR awareness, privacy?by?design, responsible AI principles (bias/fairness, interpretability), ML validation and monitoring practices, incident response for AI, and operational security. Technical training for ML engineers covers model interpretability, adversarial robustness and data?quality controls.

column1: 4.1.2 Who are the individuals involved in this training ?
column2: Product managers, data scientists, ML engineers, QA/validation teams, DevOps/operations engineers, privacy and legal teams, security engineers, and customer?facing support/ops staff receive role?appropriate training.
column23: Product managers, data scientists, ML engineers, QA/validation teams, DevOps/operations engineers, privacy and legal teams, security engineers, and customer?facing support/ops staff receive role?appropriate training.

column1: 4.1.3 Have these individuals been made aware of the legal, technical, ethical, and moral issues related to AI systems? if no, please explain why.
column2: Yes. Training curricula explicitly cover legal/compliance obligations (GDPR, contractual DPAs), ethical/responsible AI topics (bias, transparency), technical limitations (robustness, adversarial risks) and human oversight practices. Attendance and completion are tracked and recorded.
column23: Yes. Training curricula explicitly cover legal/compliance obligations (GDPR, contractual DPAs), ethical/responsible AI topics (bias, transparency), technical limitations (robustness, adversarial risks) and human oversight practices. Attendance and completion are tracked and recorded.

column1: V.                    AI MODEL TRAINING & TESTING
5.1 Has the learning data been sourced and processed in a lawful manner, and in particular:
column4: Yes. Training datasets are drawn from licensed third?party sources, public-domain datasets, synthetic data, and internal proprietary datasets used under contract and legal review. All dataset sourcing is subject to legal and IP review, data?use licensing checks, and privacy assessments. Customer data supplied by AXA is processed only under AXA’s instruction per the DPA and applicable law.

column1: 5.1.1 Is training data reused from an internal or publicly available database or specifically collected for this purpose? Please describe.
column2: Combination of sources: (a) internally curated/annotated proprietary datasets, (b) licensed third?party datasets, publicly available datasets, and (d) synthetic or generated data for augmentation and privacy-preserving use cases. Customer (AXA) production data is not repurposed for model training without explicit contractual consent.
column23: Combination of sources: (a) internally curated/annotated proprietary datasets, (b) licensed third?party datasets, publicly available datasets, and (d) synthetic or generated data for augmentation and privacy-preserving use cases. Customer (AXA) production data is not repurposed for model training without explicit contractual consent.

column1: 5.1.2 If publicly available data was used, has it been studied to determine the presence of bias?
column2: Yes. Public datasets undergo bias and representativeness reviews prior to use. This includes checking demographic coverage and running fairness metrics and statistical analyses; identified issues result in mitigation (resampling, weighting, additional data collection, or exclusion).
column23: Yes. Public datasets undergo bias and representativeness reviews prior to use. This includes checking demographic coverage and running fairness metrics and statistical analyses; identified issues result in mitigation (resampling, weighting, additional data collection, or exclusion).

column1: 5.1.3 how do you comply with copyright laws regarding the use of training data ?
column2: Compliance steps include: sourcing only licensed or public?domain data (or data used under explicit license), legal review of dataset terms, recordkeeping of licenses and provenance, and excluding copyrighted material where rights are not available. Use of customer content is governed by the DPA and Product Development Schedule; any use for product improvement is anonymized/aggregated and permitted only per contractual terms.
column23: Compliance steps include: sourcing only licensed or public?domain data (or data used under explicit license), legal review of dataset terms, recordkeeping of licenses and provenance, and excluding copyrighted material where rights are not available. Use of customer content is governed by the DPA and Product Development Schedule; any use for product improvement is anonymized/aggregated and permitted only per contractual terms.

column2: No
column3: SAP (including Joule) processes AXA personal data only as a processor under AXA’s instructions per the DPA. Any product?improvement analyses derived from customer data are anonymized/aggregated and performed only in accordance with the Product Development Schedule and applicable contract terms.
column23: No

column1: 5.2.2 If no, Please explain why.
column2: NA
column23: NA

column1: 5.2.1 If yes, Please explain why.
column2: Processing is limited by contractual obligations (DPA), privacy law, and internal policy: AXA is the controller and SAP acts as processor. Customer personal data is segregated and not used to retrain or improve models for SAP’s own purposes unless explicit contractual consent is provided and appropriate de?identification measures are applied.

Customer personal data is segregated and not used to retrain or improve models for SAP’s own purposes unless explicit contractual consent is provided and appropriate de?identification measures are applied.

column1: 5.3 What processes are in place to monitor the handling of training data, particularly regarding re-identification risk assessments and other related safeguards?
column2: Data handling governance includes documented ingestion and data?classification pipelines, automated PII detection and redaction, role?based access controls (least privilege), encryption in transit and at rest, immutable logging and audit trails, and change management. Re?identification risk is assessed during data preparation via documented risk assessment processes (provenance checks, attempts to link identifiers, uniqueness checks). Remediations include stronger anonymization, aggregation, removal of quasi?identifiers, and limiting retention/access. Periodic reviews and audits validate continued compliance.
column23: Data handling governance includes documented ingestion and data?classification pipelines, automated PII detection and redaction, role?based access controls (least privilege), encryption in transit and at rest, immutable logging and audit trails, and change management. Re?identification risk is assessed during data preparation via documented risk assessment processes (provenance checks, attempts to link identifiers, uniqueness checks). Remediations include stronger anonymization, aggregation, removal of quasi?identifiers, and limiting retention/access. Periodic reviews and audits validate continued compliance.

column1: 5.3.1 How Does the data sets used for training satisfy the minimization principle? (Only data that is necessary has been used). If it does not satisfy this principle, please explain why.
column2: Data minimization is enforced by: (a) defining clear data?purpose statements per dataset, (b) selecting only fields required for the model task, removing or masking unnecessary identifiers, (d) retaining data only for defined retention windows, and (e) reviewing datasets during intake to reject unnecessary or high?risk fields.
column23: Data minimization is enforced by: (a) defining clear data?purpose statements per dataset, (b) selecting only fields required for the model task, removing or masking unnecessary identifiers, (d) retaining data only for defined retention windows, and (e) reviewing datasets during intake to reject unnecessary or high?risk fields.

column1: 5.3.2 Is the training data anonymized or pseudonymized ?
column2: Yes
column3: Depending on source and use case. Customer/production data intended for any analytics or improvement is pseudonymized or anonymized before reuse; proprietary/licensed/synthetic datasets are used per their privacy profile.
column23: Yes

column1: 5.3.2.1. If yes, by what means? If no, please explain why.
column2: Typical techniques: automated PII detection and redaction (regex, ML-based detectors), hashing or one?way tokenization for pseudonymization, removal of direct identifiers, generalization/aggregation of quasi?identifiers, masking, and where applicable producing fully anonymized aggregate datasets. Provenance and transformation steps are documented for each dataset.
column23: Typical techniques: automated PII detection and redaction (regex, ML-based detectors), hashing or one?way tokenization for pseudonymization, removal of direct identifiers, generalization/aggregation of quasi?identifiers, masking, and where applicable producing fully anonymized aggregate datasets. Provenance and transformation steps are documented for each dataset.

column1: 5.3.3 Has the risk of reidentification been assessed? If no, please explain why.
column2: Yes. Re?identification risk assessments are performed during dataset preparation and before any dataset is accepted for training/analysis. Assessments include uniqueness tests, linkage-scenario analysis, and review of external data availability that could enable re?identification. Identified residual risk is reduced via stronger anonymization, suppression, or exclusion.
column23: Yes. Re?identification risk assessments are performed during dataset preparation and before any dataset is accepted for training/analysis. Assessments include uniqueness tests, linkage-scenario analysis, and review of external data availability that could enable re?identification. Identified residual risk is reduced via stronger anonymization, suppression, or exclusion.

column1: 5.4 Are the training data set and production data set distinctly processed ? If no, explain why.
5.5 What testing methodologies have been employed to evaluate the AI systems and AI techniques in use ?
column2: Evaluation approaches include: train/validation/test splits and cross?validation; standard performance metrics (accuracy, precision/recall, F1, AUC as applicable); calibration and confidence estimation; fairness and bias metrics across sensitive attributes; adversarial and robustness testing; scenario and edge?case testing; human review for generative outputs; controlled A/B experiments or canary rollouts in production; and ongoing production monitoring (drift detection, error rates, feedback loops).
column23: Evaluation approaches include: train/validation/test splits and cross?validation; standard performance metrics (accuracy, precision/recall, F1, AUC as applicable); calibration and confidence estimation; fairness and bias metrics across sensitive attributes; adversarial and robustness testing; scenario and edge?case testing; human review for generative outputs; controlled A/B experiments or canary rollouts in production; and ongoing production monitoring (drift detection, error rates, feedback loops).

column1: 5.6 Has the algorithm used been tested by independent third parties ?
column2: Where required, SAP engages independent assessments (security penetration testing, privacy and risk reviews, and independent model or algorithmic audits). If AXA requests a specific independent algorithmic test or third?party audit, SAP can coordinate such assessments under contract and NDA; please indicate if AXA requires a formal independent algorithmic audit so we can arrange scope and evidence.
column23: Where required, SAP engages independent assessments (security penetration testing, privacy and risk reviews, and independent model or algorithmic audits). If AXA requests a specific independent algorithmic test or third?party audit, SAP can coordinate such assessments under contract and NDA; please indicate if AXA requires a formal independent algorithmic audit so we can arrange scope and evidence.

column1: VI.                    EXPLAINABILITY & TRANSPARENCY

6.1 How do you ensure that the AI system doesn’t include any bias?
What steps have you taken to ensure explainability of the outputs resulting from the AI system or tool being used to provide a product or service to AXA?
column2: We apply dataset representativeness checks, fairness metrics, and bias?mitigation techniques during data preparation and model development. Explainability is provided via model cards, feature?importance/post?hoc methods (e.g., SHAP/LIME where appropriate), per?inference confidence scores, and logged inputs/outputs with model versioning to enable post?hoc reconstruction. High?risk or ambiguous outputs route to human review.
column23: We apply dataset representativeness checks, fairness metrics, and bias?mitigation techniques during data preparation and model development. Explainability is provided via model cards, feature?importance/post?hoc methods (e.g., SHAP/LIME where appropriate), per?inference confidence scores, and logged inputs/outputs with model versioning to enable post?hoc reconstruction. High?risk or ambiguous outputs route to human review.

column1: 6.1.1. Have the assumptions made about the training data been discussed, clearly documented, and tested against reality?
column2: Yes. Assumptions (coverage, labeling rules, intended inputs) are documented in dataset provenance records and model design docs and validated via sampling, label QC, and pilot testing against production?like data distributions.
column23: Yes. Assumptions (coverage, labeling rules, intended inputs) are documented in dataset provenance records and model design docs and validated via sampling, label QC, and pilot testing against production?like data distributions.

column1: 6.1.2. Has a bias study been conducted? According to which method? Ex: Adverse Impact Ratio, fairness metrics, statistical tests, game theory, Shapley, LIME., etc.
column2: Yes. Bias evaluations are conducted using standard fairness metrics and statistical tests appropriate to the task (e.g., group?level metrics, adverse impact ratio, equality of odds where applicable) and supplemented with explainability tools (SHAP/LIME) to identify feature?level drivers of disparate outcomes.
column23: Yes. Bias evaluations are conducted using standard fairness metrics and statistical tests appropriate to the task (e.g., group?level metrics, adverse impact ratio, equality of odds where applicable) and supplemented with explainability tools (SHAP/LIME) to identify feature?level drivers of disparate outcomes.

column1: 6.1.3. If bias was identified, what steps were taken to reduce it? Can output be explained to ensure any bias is identified?
column2: Mitigations include resampling/augmentation, re?weighting, feature removal or transformation, threshold adjustments, and targeted additional data collection. Outputs are explainable via logged metadata, confidence scores, and explainability artifacts to help identify biased behavior; persistent or unresolved bias triggers human?in?the?loop review and remediation plans.
column23: Mitigations include resampling/augmentation, re?weighting, feature removal or transformation, threshold adjustments, and targeted additional data collection. Outputs are explainable via logged metadata, confidence scores, and explainability artifacts to help identify biased behavior; persistent or unresolved bias triggers human?in?the?loop review and remediation plans.

column1: 6.2. Please give details of the transparency and information provisions you have in place as as per the requirements of the Art. 13 of the EU AI Act (and art. 50 if applicable).
column2: We provide clear machine?generated content disclosures in user interfaces and documentation, publish model cards and limitations, log decision?relevant metadata (model version, confidence, input hash) for auditability, and cooperate with AXA to supply required transparency information to data subjects per contract and applicable law.
column23: We provide clear machine?generated content disclosures in user interfaces and documentation, publish model cards and limitations, log decision?relevant metadata (model version, confidence, input hash) for auditability, and cooperate with AXA to supply required transparency information to data subjects per contract and applicable law.

column1: VII.                    KEEPING MODEL DATA ACCURATE
7.1 Has the accuracy of the data been verified and how is it controlled? If no, please justify.
column2: Yes. Data accuracy is verified via schema validation, automated checks (consistency, range checks), label QC sampling, and human annotation review. Invalid or low?quality data is rejected or corrected before training.
column23: Yes. Data accuracy is verified via schema validation, automated checks (consistency, range checks), label QC sampling, and human annotation review. Invalid or low?quality data is rejected or corrected before training.

column1: 7.1.1 Are the data used representative of the data observed in the production environment?  If no, please justify.
column2: Yes. Representativeness is assessed by comparing training/validation distributions with production samples; gaps are addressed by targeted data collection or augmentation to align distributions.
column23: Yes. Representativeness is assessed by comparing training/validation distributions with production samples; gaps are addressed by targeted data collection or augmentation to align distributions.

column1: 7.1.2  What methodology is used to guarantee representativeness of the training data?
column2: Methodology includes distributional comparisons (feature histograms, KS tests), stratified sampling to ensure coverage of key segments, targeted data sourcing for under?represented groups, and ongoing monitoring for drift after deployment.
column23: Methodology includes distributional comparisons (feature histograms, KS tests), stratified sampling to ensure coverage of key segments, targeted data sourcing for under?represented groups, and ongoing monitoring for drift after deployment.

column1: 7.2 In the case of an AI system using continuous learning, what mechanism is implemented to ensure the quality of the data used continuously?
column2: Continuous?learning pipelines include data quality gates, schema and label validation, feedback review loops, human vetting of candidate training examples, and staging environments with validation tests before models are promoted to production.
column23: Continuous?learning pipelines include data quality gates, schema and label validation, feedback review loops, human vetting of candidate training examples, and staging environments with validation tests before models are promoted to production.

column1: 7.3 Are regular mechanisms in place to assess the risk of loss of quality or change in data distribution ?
column2: Yes
column23: Yes

column1: If yes, please explain how it works.
column2: Production monitoring includes drift detection (statistical tests on input/output distributions), performance monitoring (error rates, confidence trends), and alerting thresholds that trigger investigation, rollback, or retraining workflows.
column23: Production monitoring includes drift detection (statistical tests on input/output distributions), performance monitoring (error rates, confidence trends), and alerting thresholds that trigger investigation, rollback, or retraining workflows.

column1: If no, please justify.
column2: NA
column23: NA

column1: 7.4 Do the quality control and maintenance metrics for the data collected during use of the AI system in the production environment correspond with the quality and maintenance controls established with the training and validation data ?
column2: Yes
column23: Yes

column1: If yes, please explain how it works.
column2: Production metrics (data quality, input distribution, label quality from feedback) are aligned with training/validation controls and compared periodically; discrepancies trigger investigation and remediation.
column23: Production metrics (data quality, input distribution, label quality from feedback) are aligned with training/validation controls and compared periodically; discrepancies trigger investigation and remediation.

column1: If no, please justify.
column2: NA
column23: NA

column1: 7.5 Is the quality of the AI system's output monitored during the AI system's life cycle ?
column2: Yes
column23: Yes

column1: If yes, please explain how it works.
column2: Output quality is monitored via automated metrics (accuracy, precision/recall, confidence calibration), A/B/canary deployments, user/annotator feedback ingestion, periodic model performance reviews, and triggers for retraining or rollback when thresholds are breached.
column23: Output quality is monitored via automated metrics (accuracy, precision/recall, confidence calibration), A/B/canary deployments, user/annotator feedback ingestion, periodic model performance reviews, and triggers for retraining or rollback when thresholds are breached.

column1: If no, please justify.
column2: NA
column23: NA

column1: VIII.                    LOGS
8.1. Is action logging in place ? Does it cover changes made to the AI system, software or hardware, requests sent to it, data input and output to the system ?
column2: Yes. Comprehensive logs record administrative changes (config/releases), inference requests (metadata or hashed inputs), outputs, model version, timestamps, and operator actions.
column23: Yes. Comprehensive logs record administrative changes (config/releases), inference requests (metadata or hashed inputs), outputs, model version, timestamps, and operator actions.

column1: 8.2. Is there an automatic log analysis?
column2: Yes. Logs are forwarded to centralized monitoring/SIEM where automated analyses run to detect anomalies, trends, and suspicious patterns; alerts are generated for investigation.
column23: Yes. Logs are forwarded to centralized monitoring/SIEM where automated analyses run to detect anomalies, trends, and suspicious patterns; alerts are generated for investigation.

column1: 8.3. Would it allow the identification of attempted attacks such as membership inference or model poisoning (especially in the case of continuous learning)? If yes, how would the system respond to detected attacks?
column2: Yes
column23: Yes

column1: If no, please justify.
column2: NA
column23: NA

column1: If yes, how would the system respond to detected attacks?
column2: Monitoring is designed to detect anomalies indicative of attacks (e.g., unusual query patterns, sudden distributional shifts). Response procedures include isolating affected models, pausing continuous?learning ingestion, forensic log review, rollback to known good models, and initiating incident response and remediation.
column23: Monitoring is designed to detect anomalies indicative of attacks (e.g., unusual query patterns, sudden distributional shifts). Response procedures include isolating affected models, pausing continuous?learning ingestion, forensic log review, rollback to known good models, and initiating incident response and remediation.

column1: 8.4 Is there a log of the elements (data used for inference, confidence indicators, system version, etc.) used for decision making by the AI system ?
column2: Yes. Decision?relevant elements (inference metadata, model version, confidence scores, timestamps, redacted or hashed inputs) are logged to support auditability.
column23: Yes. Decision?relevant elements (inference metadata, model version, confidence scores, timestamps, redacted or hashed inputs) are logged to support auditability.

column1: 8.4.1 Can the logged elements be used to explain, after the fact, a particular decision made by the AI system?
column2: Yes
column3: Logged metadata plus explainability outputs (feature importance / post?hoc traces) enable after?the?fact reconstruction and explanation of decisions.
column23: Yes

column1: 8.4.2. How long is this information kept for?
column2: Retention follows contractual terms and internal retention policies; retention periods can be configured per contract.
column23: Retention follows contractual terms and internal retention policies; retention periods can be configured per contract.

column1: 8.4.3 Are they limited to the strict categories necessary to explain the decision?
column2: Yes
column3: Logs contain the minimum metadata required for traceability and explanation; sensitive personal data is redacted or hashed where appropriate and retained only as permitted by contract and law.
column23: Yes

column1: IX.                    QUALITY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
9.1. If you answered 'yes' to Q3.1, please give details of the Quality Management System you have in place, as per the requirements of Art. 17 of the EU AI Act.
column2: Not applicable: Joule is not classified as a high?risk AI system under current criteria. Regardless, we maintain a product QMS aligned to secure SDLC and ML lifecycle best practices (requirements traceability, testing, change control, validation, monitoring), and can map these to Art.17 requirements if needed.
column23: Not applicable: Joule is not classified as a high?risk AI system under current criteria. Regardless, we maintain a product QMS aligned to secure SDLC and ML lifecycle best practices (requirements traceability, testing, change control, validation, monitoring), and can map these to Art.17 requirements if needed.

column1: 9.3. What measures do you take to ensure the monitoring and maintenance of your AI model? Are there periodic assessments to evaluate the model’s performance, reliability, and safety over time?
column2: Measures include continuous monitoring (performance, drift, error trends), scheduled periodic model reviews, canary rollouts, retraining pipelines with validation gates, security and privacy scans, and documented maintenance schedules and review checklists.
column23: Measures include continuous monitoring (performance, drift, error trends), scheduled periodic model reviews, canary rollouts, retraining pipelines with validation gates, security and privacy scans, and documented maintenance schedules and review checklists.

column1: X.                    HUMAN OVERSIGHT

10.1. Please describe the existing human supervision processes for your AI system? How are 'human-in-the-loop' mechanisms integrated to ensure effective oversight and facilitate intervention when necessary?
column2: Human?in?the?loop is used for high?risk, low?confidence, or ambiguous cases: workflows route flagged results to reviewers, reviewers can override or correct outputs, and corrections feed back into supervised retraining pipelines. SOPs define escalation paths and responsibilities.
column23: Human?in?the?loop is used for high?risk, low?confidence, or ambiguous cases: workflows route flagged results to reviewers, reviewers can override or correct outputs, and corrections feed back into supervised retraining pipelines. SOPs define escalation paths and responsibilities.

column1: 10.1.1 If such mechanisms are not integrated, is it feasible to establish human oversight by assigning human operators? If so, would your company be responsible for managing and providing this human supervision ?
column2: Mechanisms are integrated; where additional human oversight is requested, we can configure workflows and provide managed human review services under contract.
column23: Mechanisms are integrated; where additional human oversight is requested, we can configure workflows and provide managed human review services under contract.

column1: XI.                    RISK MANAGEMENT SYSTEM

11.1 Could you provide us with documentation outlining your Risk Management System and processes for identifying, assessing, mitigating, and monitoring risks associated with your AI system? column23: Yes. We maintain AI/ML risk assessment templates, lifecycle risk registers, and mitigation plans. Documents can be provided to AXA under contractual confidentiality terms.

SLA and notification timelines are defined in the DPA/contract.

column1: 11.3.1. If Yes, could you please describe the risk?
column2: Residual risks (e.g., low?probability model errors, rare distributional edge cases, small re?identification risk in aggregated analytics) are documented and accepted with mitigation plans and monitoring in place.
column3: Examples: (a) rare misclassification on atypical receipts causing incorrect downstream actions; (b) residual bias in niche subpopulations despite mitigation; © small theoretical re?identification risk in aggregated datasets — each has documented controls (human review, exclusion rules, further anonymization).
column23: Residual risks (e.g., low?probability model errors, rare distributional edge cases, small re?identification risk in aggregated analytics) are documented and accepted with mitigation plans and monitoring in place.

column1:
16.3 If your solution involves generative AI, could you please describe your safeguards against misuse and manipulation?
Guidance note: Generative AI are AI Systems used to generate content (text, image, sound, video, etc.) (Art. 3(1)(i)) AI Act).
column2: Safeguards: input sanitization/prompt filtering, content moderation filters, rate limits and quotas, provenance metadata and disclaimers, configurable redaction of sensitive content, human review for high?risk outputs, and monitoring for misuse patterns.
column23: Safeguards: input sanitization/prompt filtering, content moderation filters, rate limits and quotas, provenance metadata and disclaimers, configurable redaction of sensitive content, human review for high?risk outputs, and monitoring for misuse patterns.

column1: 16.3.1 What cybersecurity measures are in place to protect your AI model?
column2: Cybersecurity measures: role?based access control, encryption in transit and at rest, key management policies, network segmentation, vulnerability management, regular pen tests, secure SDLC practices, and incident response procedures.
column23: Cybersecurity measures: role?based access control, encryption in transit and at rest, key management policies, network segmentation, vulnerability management, regular pen tests, secure SDLC practices, and incident response procedures.

column1: 16.3.2 How do you ensure the authenticity and traceability of outputs generated by your generative AI?
column2: We log model version, input hashes, output metadata, and timestamps; include provenance disclosures and, where required, embed metadata or watermarking strategies and maintain audit trails to support traceability and provenance verification.
column23: We log model version, input hashes, output metadata, and timestamps; include provenance disclosures and, where required, embed metadata or watermarking strategies and maintain audit trails to support traceability and provenance verification.

column1: 16.4 How do you ensure transparency about the capabilities and limitations of your AI system for downstream providers and end-users ?
column2: Through model cards, user documentation and disclosures, UI notices about AI use and limitations, publishing performance metrics and known failure modes, and contractual documentation describing capabilities, constraints and intended uses.
column23: Through model cards, user documentation and disclosures, UI notices about AI use and limitations, publishing performance metrics and known failure modes, and contractual documentation describing capabilities, constraints and intended uses.
